salary.lateAlertDesc: Based on your payday setting (every {{ordinal}} of the month, +3 day grace period).
salary.paydayNote: Payments arriving more than 3 days after this date are flagged as late.
salary.emptyDesc: Record your first salary payment to get started.
salary.lateDetailsLate: Expected on the {{ordinal}} — paid {{days}} day(s) late.
salary.lateDetailsOnTime: Expected on the {{ordinal}} — paid on time or early.
salary.modalDesc: Record when and how much you were paid.

loans.illegalBannerDesc: Interest rates above {{threshold}}% per month may indicate an unlicensed lender (loan shark). In Singapore this is illegal. You are protected under the Moneylenders Act — you do not have to repay illegal loans.
loans.emptyDesc: Add a loan to track repayments and projected payoff date.
loans.neverDesc: Your payment of {{payment}} doesn't cover the monthly interest of {{interest}}. The balance will grow over time.
loans.illegalCardDesc: Licensed moneylenders in Singapore are capped at 4% per month. An interest rate of {{rate}}% per month exceeds this limit. Contact the Registry of Moneylenders or call the X-Ah Long hotline at 1800-924-5664.
loans.modalDesc: Enter the loan details to start tracking repayment.
loans.lenderPlaceholder: e.g. OCBC, DBS, or lender's name
loans.rateWarning: ⚠ Rate above 4%/month — this may be an illegal loan.
loans.errorRate: Enter a valid rate (0 or above)

scams.emergencyBanner: 🚨 If you are in danger call 999  ·  MOM Helpline: 1800-333-1313
scams.pageSubtitle: Latest scams targeting workers in Singapore
scams.sourceNote: Alerts are based on reports from MOM, SPF, and the Scam Alert Singapore feed. Always verify via official channels.

report.title: Report a Scam
report.subtitle: Your report helps protect other workers. You are not in trouble.
report.whatPlaceholder: Describe the scam — what did they say, what did they ask you to do?
report.contactNote: Only used if we need to follow up with you. Not shared publicly.
report.policeDesc: Filing a report with us does not replace a police report. For scams involving money lost, file an official report at police.gov.sg/e-services/make-e-report or call 1800-255-0000.
report.successDesc: Thank you for reporting. Your information helps us warn other workers. If you are in immediate danger, call 999.

alerts.title: Fake MOM Officers Demanding Levy Payments
alerts.description: Scammers are calling workers and employers claiming to be Ministry of Manpower officers. They say there are unpaid foreign worker levies and demand immediate bank transfer or PayNow payment to avoid arrest. Real MOM officers never ask for payments over the phone.
whatToDo: Hang up immediately — MOM will never call you to demand payment by phone.
Do not transfer any money, even if they threaten arrest.
Call MOM directly on 6438 5122 to verify any official matter.
Report the call to the Police at 1800-255-0000.

alerts.title: WhatsApp Phishing: Fake DBS / POSB Login Links
alerts.description: A WhatsApp message claiming to be from DBS Bank tells workers their account is suspended. It includes a link to a fake DBS login page that steals username, password, and OTP codes. The messages look real with the DBS logo. Several workers have lost savings after clicking the link.
whatToDo: Never click bank links sent by WhatsApp, SMS, or email.
Always type your bank URL directly into the browser.
If you entered your details, call DBS immediately at 1800-111-1111 to freeze your account.
Report to ScamShield: call 1799 or use the ScamShield app.

alerts.title: Fake Job Offers Asking for Upfront Placement Fees
alerts.description: Facebook and Telegram groups are advertising high-paying construction and cleaning jobs in Singapore, but asking workers to pay S$200–S$800 as a placement fee or work permit processing fee before the job starts. After payment, the job agent disappears. Licensed employment agencies in Singapore are not allowed to charge job seekers.
whatToDo: Never pay any fee to get a job — this is illegal in Singapore.
Check if the agency is licensed at mom.gov.sg/employment-agencies-search.
If you have already paid, report to MOM Taskforce at 1800-333-1313.
Keep all receipts and messages as evidence.

alerts.title: Illegal Moneylenders Offering Instant Loans via SMS
alerts.description: Unlicensed moneylenders are sending unsolicited SMS and WhatsApp messages advertising instant cash loans with no checks required. They charge interest rates far above the legal 4% monthly cap, and use threats, harassment, and property damage against borrowers and their families. Borrowing from unlicensed lenders is a criminal offence in Singapore.
whatToDo: Do not borrow from anyone who contacts you by SMS or WhatsApp — all licensed lenders are listed at moneylenders.justice.gov.sg.
If threatened or harassed, call Police immediately at 999.
Report to the X-Ah Long hotline: 1800-924-5664 (available 24/7).
You are a victim — Singapore law protects you even if you have already borrowed.

alerts.title: Fake Dormitory Agents Collecting Advance Rent
alerts.description: Scammers posing as dormitory agents are collecting 1–3 months advance rent from workers seeking accommodation near their worksite. The dormitory does not exist or is not under the agent's control. Workers arrive to find no room and lose several hundred dollars. Always verify dormitory details with your employer or the BCA-registered dormitory operator directly.
whatToDo: Never pay advance rent before viewing the room and confirming the agent is authorised by the dormitory.
Check BCA-registered dormitories at bca.gov.sg/workersdormitory.
Pay only by PayNow or cheque to the official dormitory company — not to a personal account.
If scammed, file a Police report at any Neighbourhood Police Centre or at police.gov.sg/e-services/make-e-report.

loanshark.pageSubtitle: Check which statements apply to identify an illegal lender
loanshark.infoNote: In Singapore, licensed moneylenders are regulated by the Registry of Moneylenders under the Moneylenders Act. Interest is capped at 4% per month. Unlicensed lending is a criminal offence — and so is harassing borrowers.
loanshark.legalTitle: What a Legal Moneylender Looks Like
loanshark.legalDesc: A licensed moneylender in Singapore is registered, regulated, and bound by the Moneylenders Act. Here is what they must do:
loanshark.verifyNote: Verify any moneylender at moneylenders.justice.gov.sg before borrowing. The full list of licensed lenders is public and free to check.
loanshark.disclaimer: Information based on the Moneylenders Act (Cap. 188) and MOM guidelines. Not legal advice.

flags.label: They contacted you first — by WhatsApp, SMS, or social media
flags.detail: Licensed moneylenders in Singapore are prohibited by law from advertising via SMS, WhatsApp, phone calls, or social media. If they reached out to you, they are not licensed.

flags.label: They cannot provide a license number or refuse to show it
flags.detail: Every licensed moneylender must be registered with the Registry of Moneylenders. You can verify any license number at moneylenders.justice.gov.sg. Refusing to provide one is a serious red flag.

flags.label: Interest rate is above 4% per month
flags.detail: Singapore law caps interest at 4% per month for all licensed moneylenders, regardless of your income or loan size. Any rate above this is illegal.

flags.label: They asked for your SingPass login or password
flags.detail: No legitimate lender, bank, or government agency will ever ask for your SingPass credentials. This is identity theft. Do not provide them under any circumstances.

flags.label: They asked for your internet banking login, ATM PIN, or bank card
flags.detail: Licensed lenders disburse loans directly to your bank account — they have no reason to access your account themselves. Handing over banking access exposes all your savings.

flags.label: No written loan contract was provided, or it was blank / incomplete
flags.detail: The Moneylenders Act requires a signed loan contract before any money changes hands. A legitimate lender will give you time to read it. Never sign a blank or partially filled form.

flags.label: They asked for an upfront fee or deposit before releasing the loan
flags.detail: Licensed lenders may only deduct an administrative fee (max 10% of principal) from the loan amount itself — never as an upfront cash payment before you receive anything.

flags.label: They have no physical office, or the office feels unofficial / residential
flags.detail: All licensed moneylenders must operate from a registered commercial office address. If there is no office, or they only meet you in coffeeshops or via phone, they are not licensed.

flags.label: They threatened you, your family, or your employer
flags.detail: Threats, harassment, property damage (splashing paint, locking doors), and contacting your employer are criminal offences in Singapore. You are a victim — call 999 immediately.

flags.label: They kept your passport, Work Permit, or IDs as collateral
flags.detail: It is illegal for anyone to retain your travel or identity documents. Licensed moneylenders are expressly prohibited from doing this. Report to MOM or Police immediately.

green.label: Likely Legitimate
green.summary: No red flags selected. This may be a licensed moneylender. Always verify their license number at moneylenders.justice.gov.sg before signing anything.

yellow.label: Be Cautious
yellow.summary: Some warning signs are present. Do not proceed until you have verified the lender's license number and received a full written contract. Consider calling Credit Counselling Singapore for advice.

red.label: Likely Illegal — Do Not Proceed
red.summary: Multiple serious red flags indicate this is very likely an unlicensed moneylender (loan shark). Do not borrow, do not pay any fees, and do not hand over any documents or passwords. You are protected by law.

legalFacts: Has a registered commercial office you can visit in person
Provides a full written loan contract before any money changes hands
Shows you their Registry of Moneylenders licence number (verifiable online)
Charges no more than 4% interest per month on the outstanding balance
Does not contact you via WhatsApp, SMS, or social media — you go to them
Never asks for your SingPass, banking login, ATM card, or documents as collateral
Deducts any admin fee from the loan — never asks for upfront cash before disbursement

helpContacts.note: Anonymous reporting. You will not get in trouble for calling.
Call immediately if you are threatened, harassed, or in danger.
Verify a moneylender licence or report an illegal lender.
For work permit issues, employment disputes, and general assistance.
Free debt counselling. They help you find legal ways to manage debt.

chat.emptySubtitle: Ask me about budgeting, sending money home, loan sharks, or your rights as a worker in Singapore.
suggested: How do I send money home cheaply?
chat.errorMsg: Sorry, something went wrong. Please try again.
chat.disclaimer: For guidance only. Call 999 in emergencies.
chat.noApiKey: API key not configured. Add VITE_ANTHROPIC_API_KEY to your .env file.

scamQuiz.perfectBadge: Perfect score! You're scam-aware. Help a friend take the quiz.

ratings.label: You need to be careful
ratings.desc: Scammers specifically target migrant workers. Learn the warning signs below and stay safe.

ratings.label: Good awareness
ratings.desc: You know some scam tactics — but one mistake can cost you thousands. Review the tips below.

ratings.label: Scam expert!
ratings.desc: Excellent! Share what you know with your friends and help keep your community safe.

questions.scenario: A stranger messages you on WhatsApp: "Hi, I am from MOM. Your work pass has a problem. Send me your SingPass login and password now or your pass will be cancelled today."
options: Send my SingPass details quickly so I don't lose my job
This is a scam — MOM never asks for SingPass passwords
Ask the person for their staff ID first, then decide
questions.explanation: MOM, police, banks, and government agencies will NEVER ask for your SingPass password or OTP. Hang up or ignore the message and call MOM directly at 6438-5122 to verify.

questions.scenario: A Facebook ad offers a job in Singapore paying S$4,000/month for light work. No experience needed. You just need to pay a S$300 "registration fee" to secure your spot.
options: Pay the fee — good jobs go fast
This is a job scam — legitimate employers never charge workers upfront fees
Negotiate the fee down to S$100 before paying
questions.explanation: Under the Employment of Foreign Manpower Act, employers cannot charge workers recruitment fees. Any job that requires you to pay money first is a scam. Report to MOM at 1800-333-1313.

questions.scenario: Someone slips a card under your dormitory door: "Need cash fast? No credit check! Borrow up to S$5,000 today. WhatsApp us." The card has no company name or licence number.
options: WhatsApp them — it's convenient and I need money urgently
Ignore it — this is an unlicensed loan shark (Ah Long)
Borrow a small amount first to test if they're trustworthy
questions.explanation: Licensed moneylenders in Singapore advertise only through their own websites, business directories, or physical premises — never by flyers or SMS. Borrowing from loan sharks leads to harassment and violence. Call the X-Ah Long hotline: 1800-924-5664.

questions.scenario: Your employer has not paid your salary for 2 months. He says: "Don't report to MOM or I will cancel your work pass and send you home." What should you do?
options: Wait quietly — I can't risk losing my work pass
Report to MOM — they protect workers and cannot cancel your pass for reporting
Borrow money from friends and say nothing
questions.explanation: MOM protects workers who make salary claims. Your employer CANNOT cancel your work pass just for reporting unpaid wages — that would be an additional offence. File a salary claim at mom.gov.sg or call 1800-333-1313.

questions.scenario: A new friend asks if you can receive S$8,000 into your bank account and transfer it to another account for a "small commission." He says it's for his business.
options: Agree — it's easy money and I'm just moving funds
Refuse — using your account this way is money mule fraud, which is a criminal offence
Ask for a bigger commission before agreeing
questions.explanation: Allowing others to use your bank account to move money is a criminal offence in Singapore under the Corruption, Drug Trafficking and Other Serious Crimes Act. You can be jailed up to 3 years and fined up to S$50,000, even if you didn't know the money was from crime.

questions.scenario: You receive an SMS: "DBS Bank: Unusual login detected on your account. Click this link to verify: dbs-secure-login.com." The link looks different from the official DBS website.
options: Click the link and log in quickly to protect my account
Delete the SMS — this is a phishing scam. Call DBS directly at 1800-111-1111
Forward the link to a friend to check if it's real
questions.explanation: Banks never send links asking you to log in. The real DBS site is dbs.com.sg — anything else is fake. Always type the bank's URL yourself or call the number on the back of your card. You can also call ScamShield helpline: 1799.

questions.scenario: An online "investment guru" in a Telegram group says you can double your money in 30 days by investing in a special cryptocurrency fund. Many people in the group post screenshots of their big profits.
options: Invest S$500 — the testimonials look convincing
Report the group — this is a classic investment scam with fake testimonials
Wait to see if others get paid before investing
questions.explanation: Guaranteed high returns in a short time is the #1 sign of a scam. The profit screenshots are fake, and the "group members" praising the scheme are paid scammers. Report investment scams to the police at police.gov.sg/iwitness or call 999.

questions.scenario: A landlord on Carousell asks for 3 months' rent (S$2,400) as deposit via PayNow before you can view the room. He says many people want the room and it will be gone by tonight.
options: Transfer the money fast before someone else takes the room
Refuse — never pay before viewing. This is likely a rental scam
Pay half now and half after viewing
questions.explanation: Urgency and pressure to pay before viewing are classic rental scam tactics. Always view the property in person, verify the landlord's identity and ownership, and never transfer money before signing a contract. Check scamalert.sg for reported scam listings.

emergencyFund.pageSubtitle: Know exactly how much you need — and how long to get there.
emergencyFund.savingsLabel: How much can you save each month?
emergencyFund.enterSavingsHint: Enter your monthly savings to see how long this will take.
emergencyFund.reachInOneMonth: You could reach this in 1 month!
emergencyFund.whyDesc: If you lose your job in Singapore, your work pass is cancelled and you may need to leave within 30 days. A 6-month emergency fund gives you time to find a new employer, pay for flights, or support your family while you sort things out — without borrowing from loan sharks.
emergencyFund.emptyDesc: We'll calculate how much you need and how long it will take.
emergencyFund.tipsHeading: Where to keep your emergency fund
emergencyFund.disclaimer: Deposit insurance in Singapore protects up to S$75,000 per bank per depositor via SDIC.

tips.title: POSB / DBS eMySavings
tips.body: No minimum balance, no fall-below fee. Open online with SingPass. Your money is protected by SDIC up to S$75,000.

tips.title: GXS Bank or MariBank
tips.body: Digital banks open to work-pass holders. Higher savings interest (up to ~3%), no fees, no minimum balance.

tips.title: Keep it separate
tips.body: Put your emergency fund in a different account from your spending account so you're not tempted to dip into it.

tips.title: Avoid informal savings groups (ROSCAs)
tips.body: "Chit funds" or rotating savings clubs have no legal protection. If the organiser disappears, your money is gone.

bankingGuide.pageSubtitle: How to open an account, what you need, and what to do if something goes wrong.
bankingGuide.docsTip: Tip: Get your employer to print their letter on company letterhead with a company stamp — banks often reject letters without one.
bankingGuide.recDesc: Start with a POSB Everyday Savings account — no fees, no minimum balance, and the largest ATM network. Once you have S$1,000+ saved, consider a digital bank like GXS for higher interest on your emergency fund.
bankingGuide.cardHolderHeading: If your employer holds your bank card
bankingGuide.cardHolderAlertDesc: No employer has the right to hold your bank card, ATM card, or bank passbook. Your salary belongs to you. If this is happening to you, follow the steps below.
bankingGuide.disclaimer: Information correct as of early 2025. Always verify directly with the bank before visiting.

docs.label: Work Permit card
docs.sub: The blue card from MOM — always carry it

docs.label: Employer letter
docs.sub: On company letterhead, with company stamp, confirming your employment and salary

docs.label: Address proof
docs.sub: Tenancy agreement or employer letter showing your Singapore address

banks.name: POSB Everyday Savings
banks.notes: Best for most workers. Widest ATM network in Singapore (over 1,400 machines). Accepts work permit holders.
docs: Singapore address proof (employer letter or tenancy agreement)

banks.name: DBS Multiplier Account
banks.notes: Higher interest rates if you receive salary and transact regularly. Good once you have some savings built up.
docs: Work Permit or S Pass or Employment Pass

banks.name: OCBC 360 Account
banks.notes: Bonus interest when you credit salary. Work permit holders accepted. Branch visit required to open.

banks.name: UOB One Account
banks.notes: Good interest on salary crediting. Some branches serve foreign workers — call ahead to confirm.

digital.name: GXS Bank
digital.desc: A fully digital bank open to work permit holders. No minimum balance, no fees. Offers up to 3.48% interest on savings. Open entirely through the app — no branch visit needed.

digital.name: MariBank
digital.desc: Sea Group's digital bank. No minimum balance, no fall-below fee, competitive savings rate. Open via app with your work permit details.

digital.name: YouTrip
digital.desc: Multi-currency e-wallet. Great for sending money home at low rates and paying in your home currency when shopping online. Not a full bank — no interest on balance.

digital.name: GrabPay
digital.desc: E-wallet linked to your Grab app. Accepted at thousands of merchants, hawker centres, and for GrabFood. Top up via PayNow or ATM. No bank account required to start.

digital.name: Singtel Dash
digital.requirement: Any mobile number (no bank account needed)
digital.desc: E-wallet by Singtel. Top up with cash at 7-Eleven or Cheers stores — useful if you don't have a local bank account yet. Can send money to over 30 countries.

steps.title: Know your rights
steps.body: It is illegal in Singapore for your employer to hold your bank card, ATM card, or passbook. This is covered under the Employment Act and MOM regulations.

steps.title: Ask politely first
steps.body: Tell your employer: "I need my bank card to withdraw my own salary." Most employers will return it when asked directly.

steps.title: Contact MOM
steps.body: If your employer refuses, call MOM at 1800-333-1313 (free call, Monday–Friday 8:30am–5:30pm). They can intervene on your behalf.

steps.title: Call the police if threatened
steps.body: If your employer threatens you for asking for your card back, call 999. Withholding your card by force is a criminal matter.

steps.title: Replace the card if needed
steps.body: Go to your bank branch with your work permit and passport. Tell them your card was taken. The bank will cancel the old card and issue a new one — usually free of charge.

emergency.pageSubtitle: Help lines for migrant workers in Singapore
emergency.footer: All numbers are correct as of 2026. If a number is unavailable, call MOM on 1800-333-1313 for referral.

roles.police: Emergency response, crime reporting, threats or violence
roles.mom: Work permit issues, employer disputes, salary non-payment, repatriation
roles.moneylenders: Verify licensed moneylenders, report illegal lending
roles.comcare: Social assistance, crisis support, family difficulties
roles.twc2: Unpaid wages, work injury, employer abuse, meal assistance for stranded workers
roles.irr: Support for domestic workers — emergency shelter, legal aid, counselling
roles.home: Shelter, legal support, and advocacy for migrant workers and domestic workers
roles.fast: Helpline for domestic workers — disputes, abuse, housing
roles.ccs: Free debt counselling, repayment plans, protection from loan sharks
roles.xahlong: Report loan shark harassment — anonymous, no repercussions for callers
roles.moneysense: Free financial education and guidance on legal borrowing options
roles.scamshield: Report job scams, phishing, impersonation scams — get advice on what to do
roles.antiscam: Report scam incidents, freeze transactions, recover lost funds
roles.momtaskforce: Report fake job offers, illegal placement fees, work permit fraud
